text:
Two Canadian TV hosts have reacted to Aussie breaker Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn's bizarre Olympics performance as she continues to make headlines around the world.

Canadian TV hosts have joined a chorus of spectators mocking Aussie breaker Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn for her performance at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games.

Gunn, 36, sparked heated online debate after she scored zero points for rolling in a circle on the floor, crawling around on all fours and hopping like a kangaroo on Friday.

Her unique performance drew the ire of Olympics fans and trolls alike who made a litany of memes, jokes and skits about her online.

News of her performance has even sent tongues wagging overseas, with the performance mocked by hosts on Canadian TV show The Social.

Host Andria Bain said: “Is she doing a kangaroo? Is that the can opener? What is happening here?

“I think the bigger problem here is that we were looking at these athletes and we were marvelling like, look how big, look how strong.

“We saw every type of body type, we saw incredible physical feats and then this woman came out and she made us feel like, I guess we're all Olympians, right?

“Because we can all get down on the floor and do weird stuff and pretend to yawn.”

Gunn was on Wednesday morning notably absent from the line-up of notable Olympic athletes who touched down in Sydney from Paris via Perth after the Games.

She was not on the Qantas flight from France’s capital to Australia as she will enjoy a European jaunt instead of rushing home after the sports bash.

The breaker is set to travel around Europe with her husband Samuel Free and her parents, according to 2GB.

It comes after World DanceSport Federation (WDSF) secretary Sergei Nifontov said the 36-year-old was offered mental health support due to some of the reactions to her performance.

He said the WDSF had been in direct contact with Gunn and Australian Olympic team officials to offer her support from a safeguarding officer.

"We are aware about what has happened, especially on social media, and definitely we should put the safety of the athlete, in this case, mental safety in first place," he said.

"She has us as a federation supporting her."
